meta.module: Linux Commands: Text Processing, Permissions, Processes & Networking

concepts.title: Section 3.1: The Art of Piping & Redirection

content.why_exists: Small tools doing one thing well. Piping is how you combine these tools into complex, powerful pipelines.
content.problem: You have a log file with 1 million lines. You only want to see the lines containing 'ERROR', and you want to save that result to a new file. Do you open it in a text editor? No.
content.beginner_explanation: Redirection ('>') sends output to a file instead of the screen. Piping ('|') sends the output of one command as the input to another.
content.visual_story: Think of commands as LEGO blocks. The '|' is the connector. You can build a bridge (pipeline) of any length.
step_by_step: Run 'ls > my_files.txt' - nothing on screen, it's in the file.
Run 'cat my_files.txt | grep .json' - only show the JSON files.
content.common_mistakes: Using '>' when you meant '>>'. '>' overwrites the file, '>>' appends to it. Be careful, or you'll lose data!
content.debugging_thinking: If your pipeline is empty, check each stage. Run 'cmd1', then 'cmd1 | cmd2', then 'cmd1 | cmd2 | cmd3'. Find where the data stops flowing.
content.real_workflow: Sysadmins use 'tail -f logfile | grep --line-buffered ERROR' to watch a live system for failures in real-time.
content.kernel_internals: The '|' operator tells the shell to call 'pipe()', creating a buffer in kernel memory. The shell then 'fork()'s two processes and uses 'dup2()' to map the first's stdout and the second's stdin to that buffer.

mini_labs.task: Count how many files are in /etc.

commands.explanation: Global Regular Expression Print. Filters lines based on a search string.
The 'T-junction'. Shows output on screen AND saves it to a file simultaneously.
Concatenates two files and redirects the combined stream to a third file.

concepts.title: Section 3.2: Vi/Vim - The Universal Text Editor

content.why_exists: Vi is installed on every Unix-like system. Vim is its enhanced version. You must know it because it's always available.
content.problem: You're on a remote server with no GUI. You need to edit a config file. What editor do you use?
content.beginner_explanation: Vi has two modes: Command mode (for navigation and commands) and Insert mode (for typing text). Vim is Vi Improved with syntax highlighting and plugins.

modes.command_mode: Default mode. Press 'i' to enter insert mode. Use keys like 'h/j/k/l' for navigation.
modes.insert_mode: Type text. Press 'Esc' to return to command mode.
modes.visual_mode: Select text. Press 'v' in command mode.

content.vim_vs_vi: Vi is basic. Vim adds: syntax highlighting, multiple undo, visual mode, plugins, better search/replace.
content.practical_workflow:
# Edit a config file
$ vi /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
# Press 'i' to insert
# Make changes
# Press 'Esc' then ':wq' to save and exit
content.definition: Vi is a modal text editor that comes with all Unix systems. Vim (Vi IMproved) is an enhanced version with modern features.

content.why_exists: Nano is simpler than Vi/Vim. It's good for beginners and quick edits.
content.problem: You need to edit a file but don't want to learn Vi's complex modes.
content.beginner_explanation: Nano is a simple text editor. Type text directly, use Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit. Commands are shown at the bottom.
content.advantages: Simple, intuitive, shows commands at bottom, no modes to confuse you.
content.disadvantages: Less powerful than Vim, not installed everywhere.
content.definition: Nano is a simple, user-friendly text editor that displays commands at the bottom of the screen.

content.why_exists: You need to find specific text in files or output. Grep is the standard tool.
content.problem: Find all lines containing 'ERROR' in log files across the entire system.
content.beginner_explanation: Grep searches for patterns in text. It can search files or piped input.
content.definition: Grep searches text using patterns (regular expressions) and prints matching lines.

content.why_exists: You need to process structured text (like CSV files or log entries) and extract specific columns or perform calculations.
content.problem: Extract usernames and their home directories from /etc/passwd, or sum up disk usage from 'du' output.
content.beginner_explanation: Awk processes text line by line. Each line is split into fields (columns). You can access fields as $1, $2, etc.
examples: $ awk -F: '{print $1, $6}' /etc/passwd  # Print username and home dir
$ awk '{sum += $1} END {print sum}' numbers.txt  # Sum first column
$ awk 'length($0) > 80' file.txt     # Lines longer than 80 chars

built_in_variables.NF: Number of fields in current line

content.definition: Awk is a programming language designed for text processing and data extraction.

commands.explanation: Print first column of each line.
Print first field using ':' as separator.

content.why_exists: Extract specific columns from structured text files.
content.problem: Get only the usernames from /etc/passwd, ignoring the other fields.
content.beginner_explanation: Cut extracts sections from each line of text.
examples: $ ps aux | cut -c1-80              # Truncate wide output
content.definition: Cut removes sections from each line of files.

content.why_exists: Edit text files automatically without opening them in an editor.
content.problem: Replace all occurrences of 'old_domain.com' with 'new_domain.com' in a config file.
content.beginner_explanation: Sed processes text line by line and applies editing commands.
content.definition: Sed is a stream editor that performs basic text transformations on an input stream.

content.why_exists: Convert characters in text streams (uppercase to lowercase, remove characters, etc.).
content.problem: Convert all uppercase letters to lowercase in a file.
content.beginner_explanation: Tr translates or deletes characters from standard input.
examples: $ cat file.txt | tr -s ' '          # Squeeze multiple spaces
$ echo 'hello world' | tr 'a-z' 'A-Z'  # Lower to upper
content.definition: Tr translates, squeezes, or deletes characters from standard input.

content.why_exists: Match complex text patterns, not just literal strings.
content.problem: Find all email addresses in a file, or validate phone numbers.
content.beginner_explanation: Regex uses special characters to match patterns in text.

basic_patterns.[abc]: Any of a, b, or c

examples: $ grep '^[0-9]\+$' file.txt       # Lines with only digits
content.definition: Regular expressions are patterns used to match character combinations in strings.

content.why_exists: Control who can read, write, or execute files. Security foundation.
content.problem: Make a script executable, or prevent others from reading your private files.
content.beginner_explanation: Permissions are Read (r), Write (w), Execute (x) for Owner, Group, Others.
examples: $ chmod +x script.sh               # Add execute for all
$ chmod u+x script.sh              # Add execute for owner
$ chmod go-rwx private.txt         # Remove all permissions for group and others
content.definition: Chmod changes the permissions of a file or directory.

content.why_exists: Change which user or group owns a file.
content.problem: A file was created by root but needs to be owned by a regular user.
content.beginner_explanation: Chown changes the owner and/or group of a file.
examples: $ chown alice file.txt             # Change owner to alice
$ chown alice:developers file.txt # Change owner and group
content.definition: Chown changes the user and/or group ownership of files and directories.

content.why_exists: Allow programs to run with elevated privileges when needed.
content.problem: Users need to change their passwords (requires writing to /etc/shadow), but shouldn't have direct access.
content.beginner_explanation: SUID: Program runs with owner's permissions. SGID: Program runs with group's permissions.
examples: $ chmod u+s program               # Set SUID bit
$ chmod g+s program               # Set SGID bit
content.security_risks: SUID programs are security risks if not carefully designed.
content.definition: SUID and SGID are special permission bits that allow programs to run with different privileges.

content.why_exists: Control default permissions for newly created files.
content.problem: New files are created with permissions that are too permissive.
content.beginner_explanation: Umask subtracts permissions from the default (666 for files, 777 for directories).
content.definition: Umask sets the default permissions mask for newly created files and directories.

content.why_exists: Add new users to the system.
content.problem: Need to create accounts for new employees.
content.beginner_explanation: Useradd creates new user accounts with home directories and default settings.
examples: $ useradd -m -s /bin/bash alice  # Create with home and shell
$ useradd -G developers alice    # Add to developers group
content.definition: Useradd creates a new user account.

content.why_exists: Create groups for organizing users and permissions.
content.problem: Need to create a 'developers' group for team collaboration.
content.beginner_explanation: Groupadd creates new groups.
examples: $ groupadd -g 1001 admins        # Create with specific GID
content.definition: Groupadd creates a new group.

content.why_exists: Run commands as root or other users when needed.
content.problem: Need to install software or edit system files as administrator.
content.beginner_explanation: Sudo runs a single command as root. Su switches to root user.
examples: $ sudo apt update                 # Run command as root
$ sudo -u alice command          # Run as specific user
content.difference: Sudo: temporary elevation. Su: full login as different user.
content.definition: Sudo and su allow running commands with elevated privileges.

content.why_exists: Store user account information, passwords, and group memberships.
content.problem: Need to understand user database structure.
content.beginner_explanation: /etc/passwd: User info. /etc/shadow: Passwords. /etc/group: Groups.
examples: $ cat /etc/group | grep alice   # See alice's groups
content.definition: System files that store user, password, and group information.

commands.explanation: Show user ID and group memberships.

content.why_exists: Change user passwords securely.
content.problem: Need to change password or reset user password.
content.beginner_explanation: Passwd changes passwords for users.
examples: $ sudo passwd alice              # Change alice's password
content.definition: Passwd changes user passwords and manages account locking.

content.why_exists: A Linux system is alive with hundreds of processes. You must be the conductor of this orchestra.
content.problem: An application is frozen and consuming 100% CPU. You can't close it normally. How do you force it to stop without rebooting the whole machine?
content.beginner_explanation: Every program you run is a 'Process' with a unique ID (PID). You can see them with 'ps' and stop them with 'kill'.
content.visual_story: A process is like a worker in a factory. Sometimes a worker gets stuck and blocks the conveyor belt. The 'kill' command is like the manager telling the worker: 'Stop everything and leave.'
step_by_step: Run a long task: 'sleep 1000 &'. The '&' puts it in the background.
content.common_mistakes: Thinking 'kill' always means 'delete'. Kill sends a *signal*. SIGTERM (15) asks nicely to stop; SIGKILL (9) forces it immediately.
content.debugging_thinking: Process not dying with 'kill'? It might be a 'Zombie' or stuck in an 'Uninterruptible Sleep' waiting for disk I/O.
content.real_workflow: DevOps engineers use 'pkill -u user' to quickly clear out all processes belonging to a user who is causing system lag.
content.kernel_internals: The 'kill()' syscall doesn't kill; it sends a signal. The kernel pauses the target process, pushes a signal onto its stack, and forces it to execute a signal handler.
content.performance_note: Too many background processes can cause 'context switching' overhead, slowing down the CPU as it tries to keep everyone happy.
mini_tasks: Bring it to the foreground with 'fg'.

commands.explanation: Shows EVERY process on the system with detailed CPU and Memory usage.
Real-time process monitor. Shows who is currently 'eating' the most resources.
Sends SIGKILL. The kernel terminates the process immediately. No cleanup allowed.

content.why_exists: Control which processes get more CPU time.
content.problem: A backup process is slowing down the system. Make it run with lower priority.
content.beginner_explanation: Nice values range from -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest). Lower nice = higher priority.
examples: $ nice -n 10 backup.sh            # Run with lower priority
$ renice 10 -p PID                # Change running process priority
$ renice 10 -u alice              # Change all of alice's processes
content.definition: Nice and renice adjust process scheduling priority.

content.why_exists: Manage system services (daemons) that run in the background.
content.problem: Start, stop, or check status of system services like web server or database.
content.beginner_explanation: Systemctl controls systemd services.
examples: $ systemctl enable nginx          # Start on boot
content.definition: Systemctl manages systemd services and units.

concepts.title: Section 5.4: Resource Monitoring - free, df, du

content.why_exists: Monitor system resources: memory, disk space, disk usage.
content.problem: Check if system is running out of memory or disk space.
content.beginner_explanation: Free: memory. Df: disk space. Du: directory sizes.
content.definition: Commands to monitor system memory and disk usage.

concepts.title: Section 5.5: Package Managers - apt, yum, dnf

content.why_exists: Install, update, and manage software packages.
content.problem: Install new software or update existing packages.
content.beginner_explanation: Apt (Debian/Ubuntu), Yum/Dnf (RedHat/CentOS).
examples: $ apt update && apt upgrade       # Update packages (Ubuntu)
$ yum install package             # Install package (older RedHat)
$ dnf install package             # Install package (newer RedHat)
$ apt search keyword              # Search for packages
content.definition: Package managers handle software installation and updates.

content.why_exists: Configure shell and program behavior.
content.problem: Programs can't find executables, or need custom settings.
content.beginner_explanation: Variables that affect program behavior.
content.definition: Environment variables store configuration data for programs.

content.why_exists: Run commands automatically at scheduled times.
content.problem: Need to run backups every night or system checks hourly.
content.beginner_explanation: Cron runs commands at specified times.
content.definition: Cron is a time-based job scheduler.

content.why_exists: Configure and view network interfaces.
content.problem: Check IP address, configure network settings.
content.beginner_explanation: Ifconfig shows and configures network interfaces.
examples: $ sudo ifconfig eth0 192.168.1.100  # Set IP address
content.definition: Ifconfig configures network interfaces (deprecated, use 'ip').

concepts.title: Section 6.2: Ip Command - Modern Network Management

content.why_exists: Modern replacement for ifconfig, more powerful.
content.problem: Configure IP addresses, routes, and network interfaces.
content.beginner_explanation: Ip command manages network configuration.
examples: $ ip addr show                    # Show IP addresses
$ ip route show                   # Show routing table
$ sudo ip addr add 192.168.1.100/24 dev eth0  # Add IP
content.definition: Ip is a modern networking command that replaces ifconfig and route.

content.why_exists: Securely connect to remote systems.
content.problem: Access remote servers securely.
content.beginner_explanation: SSH provides encrypted remote access.
examples: $ ssh user@hostname               # Connect to remote host
$ ssh -i key.pem user@host        # Use specific key
$ scp file.txt user@host:/path    # Copy files securely
content.definition: SSH is a protocol for secure remote login and file transfer.

content.why_exists: Query DNS servers to resolve domain names.
content.problem: Check if DNS is working or debug name resolution.
content.beginner_explanation: Dig and nslookup query DNS servers.
examples: $ dig @8.8.8.8 google.com         # Use specific DNS server
content.definition: Dig and nslookup are DNS lookup utilities.

content.why_exists: Manage routing table for network traffic.
content.problem: Configure how traffic reaches different networks.
content.beginner_explanation: Ip route manages the routing table.
examples: $ ip route show                   # Show routing table
$ sudo ip route add default via 192.168.1.1  # Add default route
content.definition: Ip route manages the kernel routing table.

content.why_exists: Control network traffic to/from the system.
content.problem: Block unwanted network connections.
content.beginner_explanation: Ufw (simple), Iptables (advanced) manage firewall rules.
examples: $ sudo ufw enable                 # Enable UFW firewall
content.definition: Ufw and iptables are firewall management tools.

content.why_exists: Transfer files between systems.
content.problem: Upload/download files to/from remote servers.
content.beginner_explanation: FTP (insecure), SFTP (secure) for file transfer.
content.definition: FTP and SFTP are protocols for file transfer over networks.
